name: Curavacas Normal Route via Callejo Grande
name_original: Curavacas desde Vidrieros — trailrunning por el Parque Natural Montaña Palentina
description: This difficult 6.4-mile trail running route to Curavacas summit offers challenging terrain and expansive views in Montaña Palentina.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Triollo are set within the Montaña Palentina Natural Park, a region characterized by high peaks, deep valleys, and diverse forests. The landscape features prominent landmarks such as Curavacas Peak, the highest point in Palencia, and the serene River Carrión flowing through the Pineda valley. This area offers varied terrain, from challenging mountain paths to more accessible riverside trails, providing a diverse environment for running. Best jogging routes around Triollo The most popular jogging route is Espigüete East Ridge and Mazobre Waterfall Loop, a 5.9 miles (9.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 23 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers views of the Mazobre Waterfall. Another top favourite among local runners is Curavacas Normal Route via Callejo Grande, a difficult 6.4 miles (10.3 km) path. This route ascends towards Curavacas Peak, a significant landmark in the region. Local runners also love the Giant of the Narrow Valley Trail, a 2.6 miles (4.3 km) trail leading through the Montaña Palentina Natural Park, often completed in about 35 minutes. Jogging around Triollo is defined by high peaks like Curavacas, deep valleys, and the diverse forests of the Montaña Palentina Natural Park. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to difficult mountain trails. The routes in Triollo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 270 runners have used komoot to explore Triollo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running routes around Triollo?
A: The running routes around Triollo are primarily set within the Montaña Palentina Natural Park, offering a diverse range of terrain. You'll find everything from challenging mountain paths with significant elevation gains to more accessible riverside trails along the serene River Carrión. The landscape features high peaks, deep valleys, and varied forests, providing a dynamic environment for runners.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or families in Triollo?
A: Yes, while many routes are challenging, there are options for various fitness levels. For a more moderate and family-friendly experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1120254479/giant-of-the-narrow-valley-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Giant of the Narrow Valley Trail&lt;/a&gt;. This 2.6-mile (4.3 km) route offers panoramic views and is a good choice for those seeking an easier run through the Montaña Palentina Natural Park.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I might see while running in Triollo?
A: Triollo is rich in natural beauty and landmarks. You can expect to see prominent features like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839685" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Curavacas Peak&lt;/a&gt;, the highest point in Palencia, and the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3487262" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Espigüete Summit&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts glacial lagoons such as Pozo Curavacas and Pozos del Ves, and the picturesque River Carrión flowing through the Pineda valley. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1118928927/espigueete-east-ridge-and-mazobre-waterfall-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Espigüete East Ridge and Mazobre Waterfall Loop&lt;/a&gt;, even offer views of the Mazobre Waterfall.

Q: Are there any circular running routes available in the Triollo area?
A: Yes, there are circular running routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1118901197/pena-prieta-y-pena-del-infierno-por-collado-del-ves-circular-trailrunning-por-el-parque-natural-montana-palentina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Prieta y Peña del infierno por collado del Ves&lt;/a&gt;, which is a challenging trail running loop through the Montaña Palentina Natural Park, covering nearly 19 km.

Q: What is the best time of year for trail running in Triollo?
A: The Montaña Palentina region around Triollo is generally excellent for outdoor activities. Spring and autumn are often ideal, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. During winter, some higher elevation trails might have snow, so it's advisable to check local conditions. Summer can be warm, but the mountain air often provides relief, especially in the mornings.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Montaña Palentina Natural Park near Triollo?
A: While the Montaña Palentina Natural Park is a fantastic place for outdoor activities, specific regulations regarding dogs on trails can vary. It's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Always check for any specific signage or local guidelines at trailheads within the Natural Park.

Q: Where can I find parking for trailheads around Triollo?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. For popular routes like those leading to Curavacas or the Giant of the Narrow Valley Trail, there are often designated parking areas in nearby villages or at the start of the trails. It's advisable to research specific trailheads in advance for the most accurate parking information, especially during peak seasons.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the trailheads from Triollo?
A: Public transport options directly to specific trailheads in the Montaña Palentina Natural Park can be limited. Triollo itself is a small municipality. While there might be regional bus services connecting larger towns, reaching more remote trail starts often requires a car. Planning your journey with a personal vehicle is generally the most flexible option for accessing the diverse running routes.

Q: Do I need any special permits to run in the Montaña Palentina Natural Park?
A: For general recreational running on established trails within the Montaña Palentina Natural Park, special permits are typically not required. However, for certain activities, large groups, or specific protected areas, it's always best to consult the official Montaña Palentina Natural Park authority for any updated regulations or permit requirements.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Triollo?
A: The running routes around Triollo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Runners often praise the breathtaking natural landscapes, the variety of terrain from challenging mountain ascents to serene valley paths, and the sense of immersion in the Montaña Palentina Natural Park. The stunning views of peaks like Curavacas and the tranquility of the area are frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any places to eat or get refreshments near the running routes in Triollo?
A: Triollo and its surrounding villages offer a few options for refreshments. While not directly on every trailhead, you can find local cafes, pubs, and restaurants in the villages within the Montaña Palentina area. For example, Vidrieros, a village often associated with routes like the Valdenievas Trail, may have establishments where you can refuel after your run.
tips: Climbing Curavacas is an exciting experience for mountain lovers. There are several routes available, such as Callejo Grande and Senda del Notario, which offer different levels of difficulty. In addition, in the area you can find glacial lagoons, such as Pozo Curavacas and Pozos del Ves, which add a special touch to the landscape.

faqs.question: Is the Curavacas from Vidrieros trail suitable for beginner trail runners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is considered difficult and is primarily suited for experienced individuals with very good fitness levels and sure-footedness. It involves significant elevation gain, extensive scree fields, and steep sections with loose stones, making it unsuitable for beginners.

faqs.question: Where is the trailhead and what are the parking options in Vidrieros?
faqs.answer: The trail starts in the village of Vidrieros. It's a popular starting point, especially in summer, so you'll find parking available in or near the village. The route begins with a comfortable dirt road known as Camino de Valdenievas.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this trail running route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse and challenging. You'll start on a dirt road (Camino de Valdenievas), transition to a narrow path through a shaded forest, and cross streams. The most significant features are the extensive 'pedrera' (scree fields) and the steep 'Callejo Grande' with loose stones and rocks, especially towards the summit. Some downhill sections can be very fun to run.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers breathtaking scenery within the Parque Natural Montaña Palentina. You'll pass through beautiful forests, and as you ascend, enjoy increasingly expansive views. From the summit of Curavacas (2524m), you'll get panoramic views of peaks like Espigüete, Pico Murcia, Peña Prieta, and even the distant Picos de Europa. At the base of Curavacas, you can also see lakes such as Pozo del Curavacas.

faqs.question: What makes the Curavacas peak unique?
faqs.answer: Curavacas is one of the most emblematic and highest peaks in the Montaña Palentina, and the tallest mountain entirely within the province of Palencia. Its distinctive silhouette dominates the region. A unique visual aspect is the greenish tone of its walls, attributed to lichens covering the conglomerate rocks, which distinguishes it from other mountains.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to trail run Curavacas from Vidrieros?
faqs.answer: The best time to trail run this route is during the summer months when the weather is generally more stable and the scree fields are less technical. In colder months, the presence of snow and ice significantly increases the difficulty, potentially requiring crampons and making the route unsuitable for less experienced individuals.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: The provided information does not explicitly state whether dogs are allowed or if there are specific leash rules for this trail within the Parque Natural Montaña Palentina. It's advisable to check with local park authorities or visitor centers for current regulations regarding pets.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or is there an entrance fee for the Parque Natural Montaña Palentina?
faqs.answer: The provided information does not mention any specific permits or entrance fees required for trail running the Curavacas route within the Parque Natural Montaña Palentina. However, it's always a good practice to verify with the park's official channels for any updated regulations before your visit.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this challenging trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty, you should bring sturdy trail running shoes with good grip, sufficient water and nutrition, and appropriate layers for changing mountain weather. Due to the risk of falling stones in certain sections, carrying a helmet is advisable, though not universally practiced. In colder months, crampons might be necessary.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route utilizes sections of several official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Camino de Valdenievas, Camino de Valdecañasenievas, Calle Barriuco, Calle de Valdenievas, and the Ruta Valle de Pineda at various points along the way.

faqs.question: Are there any specific risks or challenges to be aware of on the Curavacas ascent?
faqs.answer: Beyond the general difficulty, the main challenges include navigating extensive 'pedrera' (scree) fields, which can be cumbersome, and steep sections with loose stones and rocks, particularly in the 'Callejo Grande'. There is also a risk of falling stones, making a helmet a sensible precaution. In winter, snow and ice significantly increase the technical difficulty.
